coredata.dc:title: An electrochemiluminescence sensor for the detection of prostate protein antigen based on the graphene quantum dots infilled TiO2 nanotube arrays
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Chunyuan
Wang, Li
Luan, Feng
Zhuang, Xuming
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we prepared titanium dioxide nanotube arrays (TiO2 NTs) infilled with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to create a hybrid structure (GQD/TiO2 NTs) and applied the GQD/TiO2 NTs as an efficient electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emitter in the detection of prostate protein antigen (PSA). The TiO2 NTs were infilled with the GQDs through an impregnation method. The GQD/TiO2 NP composite electrode showed a highly stable ECL, and, when K2S2O8 was used as a co-reactant, the ECL intensity increased 6-fold compared with that of a pure TiO2 NT electrode. Herein, to exploit the application of the GQD/TiO2 NT composite as an efficient ECL emitter for biosensing, a sandwich ECL immunoassay was developed using PSA as a model. CdTe nanoparticles (NPs) modified with Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (CdTe/MNPs) served as quenchers for the ECL immunoassay. PSA could be detected using the ultrasensitive GQD/TiO2 NT platform. The decrease in the ECL intensity was linear with the log of the concentration of the PSA in the range from 1.0 fg mL−1 to 10 pg mL−1, with a lower detection limit of 1 fg mL−1 (S/N = 3). The use of the ECL immunoassay for PSA detection were used to measure in clinical human serum samples of prostate cancer patients and controls with excellent correlation to referee chemiluminescence method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Titanium dioxide nanotube arrays
Electrochemiluminescence
Prostate protein antigen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of CdTe/MNPs-labelled Ab2 and Ab1/GQD/TiO2 NT electrodes
ECL enhancement mechanism of GQD/TiO2 NTs
Analytical application for selective PSA detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21405131 , 21778047 , 21675138 ) for support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of A) TiO2 NTs, B) GQD/TiO2 NTs.

The ECL curves of the GQD/TiO2 NT electrode and the TiO2 NT electrode in 0.05 M K2S2O8 at pH 8.3 and a 600 V PMT at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. Inset: the corresponding cyclic voltammograms acquired at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1.
ECL emission from the GQD/TiO2 NT electrode under a continuously cyclic scan in 0.05 M K2S2O8 with a pH of 8.3 and a 600 V PMT at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1.

A) Cyclic ECL curves of (a) GQD/TiO2; (b) Ab1/GQD/TiO2; (c) PSA/Ab1/GQD/TiO2; and (d) CdTe/MNP/Ab2/PSA/Ab1/GQD/TiO2 in ECL detection buffer. PSA concentration: 10 pg mL−1. (B) The ECL intensity response of CdS/TiO2 NTs in 0.05 M K2S2O8 (pH 8.3) at different concentrations of PSA (fM): a, 0; b, 1; c, 50; d, 100; e, 1000; f, 5000; and g, 10,000. The inset image is the logarithmic calibration plot for PSA determination (The concentrations of the PSA (fM) are 10,000, 8000, 5000, 1000, 100, 90, 80, 70, 50, 10 and 1 respectively). The number of measurements for each error bar was 3.

Plot displaying the selectivity of the ECL immunoassay to PSA (100 fg mL−1) by comparing it to the interfering proteins at 1.0 pg mL−1: BSA, CEA, AFP, mouse IgG and the mixed sample containing 100 fg mL−1 PSA and 1.0 pg mL−1 BSA, CEA, AFP and mouse IgG. Five electrodes were used for immunoassay under the same conditions, and the error bars show the standard deviation of three replicate determinations.
A) The preparation process for the CdTe/MNP composites. B) Schematic showing the principle of the GQD/TiO2 NT electrode for PSA detection using CdTe/MNP composites as ECL quenchers.
Comparison of different ECL performances for the detection of PSA.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: g carbon nanospheres amidogen graphene (NH2-Gr) and Au nanoparticle (NP) functionalized cerium oxide (CeO2) NPs (NH2-Gr/Au@CeO2).

head.ce:title: An electrochemiluminescence sensor for the detection of prostate protein antigen based on the graphene quantum dots infilled TiO2 nanotube arrays

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University, Yantai 264005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yantai University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we prepared titanium dioxide nanotube arrays (TiO2 NTs) infilled with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to create a hybrid structure (GQD/TiO2 NTs) and applied the GQD/TiO2 NTs as an efficient electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emitter in the detection of prostate protein antigen (PSA). The TiO2 NTs were infilled with the GQDs through an impregnation method. The GQD/TiO2 NP composite electrode showed a highly stable ECL, and, when K2S2O8 was used as a co-reactant, the ECL intensity increased 6-fold compared with that of a pure TiO2 NT electrode. Herein, to exploit the application of the GQD/TiO2 NT composite as an efficient ECL emitter for biosensing, a sandwich ECL immunoassay was developed using PSA as a model. CdTe nanoparticles (NPs) modified with Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (CdTe/MNPs) served as quenchers for the ECL immunoassay. PSA could be detected using the ultrasensitive GQD/TiO2 NT platform. The decrease in the ECL intensity was linear with the log of the concentration of the PSA in the range from 1.0 fg mL−1 to 10 pg mL−1, with a lower detection limit of 1 fg mL−1 (S/N = 3). The use of the ECL immunoassay for PSA detection were used to measure in clinical human serum samples of prostate cancer patients and controls with excellent correlation to referee chemiluminescence method.

The highly enhanced ECL intensity from the graphene quantum dots infilled TiO2 nanotube arrays was reported which exhibited excellent analytical performance for detection prostate protein antigen.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: The growth of TiO2 nanotubes (NTs) by the anodic oxidation of titanium has greatly impressed a considerable number of scientists because of their high orientation, low-cost, non-toxicity, adjustable mesopore size, high internal surface area, superior physicochemical stability and high catalytic activity [1–5]. In the field of solar cells [6,7] or photoreactors [8,9], TiO2 NT composites have become an active research topic in recent years. In addition, some TiO2 NT-based electrochemiluminescence (ECL) platforms have recently been widely studied to further increase their ECL efficiency. For example, narrow-band-gap metal semiconductors, noble-metal nanoparticles and non-metal elements have been loaded onto TiO2 NTs to improve their ECL efficiency [10–12]. However, these sensitizers can lead to serious environmental and human health problems because of the participation of toxic metals in the reaction, which limits their widespread application.

As emerging semiconductor quantum dots, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are defined particles composed of few-layer graphene with lateral dimensions smaller than 100 nm [13]. GQDs have caused significant excitement because of their chemical inertness, convenient production, low toxicity and favourable biocompatibility [14]. The development of GQDs with excellent properties has triggered their use in sensing [15], bioimaging [16,17], and photovoltaics [18]. In addition, GQDs/TiO2 NT composites have been demonstrated to function as visible-light photocatalysts [19,20]. However, the literature contains few reports on the use of GQDs/TiO2 NT composites in ECL applications. In this article, we explore the possibility of putting a GQD-infilled TiO2 NT hybrid structure (GQDs/TiO2 NTs) onto the ECL platform, with the objective of developing a desirable ECL biosensing system.

The experiment results were as expected: the GQD-infilled TiO2 NT hybrid structure vastly improved the ECL intensity. Moreover, the ECL signal of the GQD/TiO2 NT composites exhibited good stability and reproducibility, which suggested that they could be used for accurate bioanalysis. Prostate-specific antigen (PSA), which has a physiological role in liquefying the seminal fluid, is a specific prostate cancer marker; research on PSA has advanced greatly in recent years [21]. Herein, to exploit the application of GQD/TiO2 NTs as an efficient ECL emitter in bioanalysis, a sandwich ECL immunoassay was developed using PSA as a model.

When the CdTe nanoparticles (NPs) modified with Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (CdTe/MNPs) were used as a quencher, the amplified quenching of ECL from a GQD/TiO2 NT electrode was obviously observed because the CdTe NPs absorbed ECL energy from the GQD/TiO2 NT electrode efficiently [9,22]. The CdTe/MNP composite was synthesized according to the method reported previously [23,24]. As Scheme 1 shows, on the basis of the as-prepared GQD/TiO2 NT composite and the high-efficiency quenching of CdTe/MNP bioconjugates, we devised a sandwich ECL immunoassay based on the ability of the CdTe/MNPs composite to quench multiple signals via the connection of secondary antibody (Ab2). The proposed immunoassay method was successfully used in the quantitative determination of PSA in human serum, and the experimental results showed good agreement with the values obtained using the chemiluminescence method. The satisfactory results confirmed that this ECL immunoassay method was credible to the clinical assay, exhibiting great promise as a reliable technique for the detection of PSA in serum samples.

PSA antigen and antibodies, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and fetoprotein (AFP) were purchased from the Huayang Zhenglong Biochemical Lab (Chengdu, China). Titanium foils (99.8%, 0.127 mm thick), mouse immunoglobulin G (IgG), bovine serum albumin (BSA), 1-ethyl-3-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl]carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). GQDs were purchased from XFNANO Material Technologic Co. Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs, ~100 nm, 5 mg mL−1) modified by amino acids were obtained from BaseLine Chromtech Research Centre (Tianjing, China). Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) with a concentration of 0.10 M and different pH values were prepared by mixing stock solutions of 0.10 M Na2HPO4 and KH2PO4. All reagents were analytical grade.

The ECL measurements were recorded using an MPI-E electrochemiluminescence analyser (Xi'an Rimax Electronics Co. Ltd., China) with the voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PTM) set at 600 V during the measurements. A three-electrode system was adopted, with a GQD/TiO2 NT electrode as the working electrode, a Pt wire as the counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was conducted with an S-3000 N scanning electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan).

The TiO2 NTs were synthesized according to a previously reported method [11]. Prior to anodization, the titanium foil (4 cm × 0.5 cm) was ultrasonically cleaned in a dilute HF solution for 5 min, followed by ultrasonic cleaning in water for 5 min. A portion of the cleaned titanium foil with an area of 0.5 cm × 0.5 cm was immersed in an electrolyte containing 1.0 M NaF and 0.5% M (NH4)2SO4 and anodized at 20 V (supplied by a DC power supply) with a platinum cathode at room temperature for 3 h. The un-anodized top part of the titanium foil served as the electrical contact. After anodization, the samples were sonicated with ultrapure water and dried in an N2 stream. The resulting amorphous TiO2 nanotubes were annealed in an O2 atmosphere at 500 °C for 1 h with heating and cooling rates of 10 °C min−1 to crystallize the tube walls and improve their stoichiometry.

GQD/TiO2 NT nanocomposites were prepared by following a literature procedure [25]. Briefly, vacuum-assisted and electrophoretic filling methods were used to prepare the heterojunction films. The TiO2 NTs were immersed into GQD aqueous solution for 5 min under vacuum conditions at room temperature, then rinsed with deionized water and dried at 60 °C. The process was repeated for 10 times. And then the electrophoretic assembly was constructed in a three-electrode cell using the GQD aqueous solution (pH 8) as an electrolyte, TiO2 NT films as a working electrode, Pt wire as a counter electrode, and an SCE as a reference electrode. A + 6 V bias potential was applied to the TiO2 NT film for 2 h. The as-prepared GQD/TiO2 NT films were rinsed with ultrapure water.

CdTe/MNPs nanocomposites were synthesized according to previous works [12]. We dissolved 0.069 g of CdCl2·2.5H2O in 25 mL of water and then injected 55 μL of MPA, followed by a 30 min purge using N2 gas. NaBH4 (0.30 g) was reacted with 0.016 g of tellurium powder in 25 mL of water under the proper conditions (using a water bath at 60 °C) to obtain an oxygen-free NaHTe solution; the fresh NaHTe solution was then added to the aforementioned Cd-containing solution under strong agitation, with a reaction temperature of 100 °C and a reaction time of 3 h. The crude products were purified by precipitation in absolute ethanol to obtain the CdTe NPs. The CdTe NCs were subsequently dispersed in 1.0 mL of 0.1 M 1-methylimidazol aqueous solution (pH 7.4) containing 12 mg of NHS and 25 mg of EDC, with a reaction temperature of room temperature and a reaction time of 1.5 h, which resulted in the activation of the carboxylate groups on the surface of CdTe NPs. After centrifugation, the particles were alternatingly washed with PBS buffer (pH 7.4) and water several times. Finally, the activated CdTe NPs were re-dispersed in 1.0 mL of the PBS buffer (pH 7.4).

The synthesized CdTe NPs were covalently bound to the amino-modified Fe3O4 NPs with EDC as the crosslinking agent in PBS (pH 7.4). NHS (10 mg mL−1) and EDC (20 mg mL−1) were separately injected into a reaction vessel and allowed to react for 20 min at room temperature. Then, 10 μL of the 5.0 mg mL−1 amino-modified Fe3O4 was added dropwise into the solution, and allowed to react at room temperature for 2 h. The CdTe/Fe3O4 nanocomposites were subsequently collected using magnetic force and then washed several times with alcohol to remove impurities. The prepared CdTe/MNPs conjugates were denoted CdTe/MNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CdTe/MNPs-labelled Ab2 and Ab1/GQD/TiO2 NT electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The process used to fabricate the ECL immunosensor is depicted in Scheme 1. First, 1 mg of the CdTe/MNPs was dispersed in 1 mL of the PBS solution by sonication to obtain a homogeneous suspension. Subsequently, 10 mg of NHS and 20 mg of EDC were injected into the solution to activate the surface carboxyl groups of the CdTe/MNPs. Before the freshly prepared CdTe/MNPs could be used, any excess EDC and NHS was removed by centrifugation in water and ethanol solution several times. After this process, the products were re-dispersed in PBS solution. To obtain CdTe/MNPs/Ab2, 150 μL of 50 μg mL−1 Ab2 was added to the CdTe/MNP solution with a reaction temperature of 4 °C and a reaction time of 12 h. To block extra active sites, BSA (3% w/v) was injected into the CdTe/MNP/Ab2 solution for another 4 h to eliminate the nonspecific binding. Finally, the CdTe/MNP/Ab2 bioconjugates were then re-dispersed in PBS (pH 7.4) and stored in a refrigerator (4 °C) prior to use.

As Scheme 1B shows, the as-synthesized GQD/TiO2 NT electrode was immersed in a solution (1 mL) of 10 mg NHS and 20 mg EDC for 1 h at 4 °C. After being washed, the electrode was mixed with 1 mL of 50 μg mL−1 Ab1 solution under stirring at 4 °C overnight. The washed Ab1/GQD/TiO2 NTs electrode was then immersed in 3% BSA for 1 h at 4 °C, washed, and then stored at 4 °C before being used for PSA detection.

The as-prepared Ab1/CdS/TiO2 NT electrode was first incubated with different concentrations of PSA for 1 h and then further incubated with 100 μL of Ab2/CdTe/MNP solution for 1 h at 37 °C. The immunosensor was thoroughly washed with PBS to remove nonspecifically adsorbed species for the subsequent ECL measurement. The as-prepared immunoassay was then used for the detection of PSA.

The SEM image of the pure TiO2 NTs ( Fig. 1A) and the resulting heterojunction films (Fig. 1B) shows that the TiO2 NTs retain their morphology after the electrophoretic assembly with the GQDs. The nanotube arrays are clearly observed. The Fig. 1B shows dark spots with diameters of approximately 10 nm, suggesting the successful loading of GQDs onto the TiO2 NTs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL enhancement mechanism of GQD/TiO2 NTs
ce:section.ce:para: The inset of Fig. 2 shows cyclic voltammograms of the GQD/TiO2 electrodes and naked TiO2 NTs in a K2S2O8 solution. Because of the reduction reaction of S2O8 2- on the electrode surface, both electrodes showed a cathodic reduction current at any potential more negative than − 1.0 V. The current at the GQDs/TiO2 NT electrode increased substantially compared with that at the naked TiO2 NT electrode, which indicates the electron transfer was greatly accelerated by the filled GQDs.

The ECL behaviours of the naked TiO2 NT electrode and the GQDs/TiO2 NT electrode in the presence of co-reactant S2O8 2- anions are shown in Fig. 2. The TiO2 NT electrode showed an ECL response at any potential more negative than − 1.2 V. Surprisingly, when the GQDs were infilled onto the TiO2 NTs, the ECL response increased by 6-fold (increase from 2433 to 17045) and the onset ECL potential moved to a more positive position by 300 mV compared with the onset ECL potential of the naked TiO2 NT electrode.

According to results reported in the literature [10–12], the possible mechanism of the ECL generated on TiO2 NTs in S2O8 2- solution is illustrated as follows:
(1) S2O8 2- + e- → SO4 2- + SO4 •-
(2) SO4 •- → SO4 2- + h+
(3) TiO2 + h+ → TiO2 (h+)
(4) TiO2 (h+) + e- → TiO2 *
(5) TiO2 * → TiO2 + hν
where SO4 •- is sulfate radical, h+ is electron hole and TiO2 * is the TiO2 excited state.

On the basis of the aforementioned mechanism, S2O8 2- would obtain an electron from the electrode and undergo the reactions shown in Eqs. (1) and (2). As shown in Eq. (3), TiO2 can act as a fixative for the hole generated in Eq. (2). Furthermore, after the GQDs are filled onto the TiO2 NTs, the electron transmission rate could be enhanced because the GQDs exhibit good electrical conductivity; thus, the [SO4 •-] produced from Eq. (1) could be increased, which in turn greatly increased the amount of h+ formed in Eq. (2). The lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level of the GQDs at the reported values (− 3.8 to 3.5 eV) is higher than the conduction band (CB) bottom (− 4.2 eV) of the TiO2 [2,25], which indicates that the electron transferred from the GQDs to the TiO2. We propose that this transfer greatly accelerated electron-hole recombination in TiO2. The net effect of the GQD-filled TiO2 NTs was acceleration of the electron injection (Eq. (1)) and the electron-hole recombination (i.e., Eq. (4)). Therefore, it efficiently enhanced the ECL performance by the as-prepared GQD/TiO2 NT electrode, as shown in Fig. 2.

We examined the stability of the GQD/TiO2 NT electrode under a continuous cyclic scan of 21 cycles ( Fig. 3). The GQD/TiO2 NT electrode sustained a high and stable ECL emission intensity. The high cycling stability and reusability of the GQD-infilled TiO2 NTs makes them a promising candidate for ECL applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application for selective PSA detection
ce:section.ce:para: PSA has been recognized as the most reliable biomarker for the early diagnosis of prostate cancer and for monitoring for recurrence [26]. PSA was used as a model for testing the ability of the GQD/TiO2 NTs to act as an ECL emitter and for the CdTe/MNPs to act as an efficient quencher in this study. A schematic of the fabrication process for the ECL immunosensor is shown in Scheme 1B. A comparison of the ECL signals in the preparation of the immunoassay electrode, together with the ECL change of the immunoassay in sandwich detection of the antigen, is shown in Fig. 4A. First, a small decrease in the ECL intensity (peak height) was observed when the capturing antibody (Ab1), the BSA and PSA were assembled on the GQDs/TiO2 electrode (curves b and c). The ECL intensity obviously decreased, which can be ascribed to the fact that proteins can increase electron-transfer resistance in the ECL process. After the Ab1/PSA/GQD/TiO2 electrode was incubated in the Ab2/CdTe/MNP conjugates solution, the ECL quenching efficiency was greater than 95%, as shown in curve d in Fig. 4A

A wide range of PSA sensing protocols based on ECL determination have been reported. Table 1 illustrates that the immunosensor described here has a lower detection limit and a broader linear range than the previously described immunosensors [27–31].

The ECL immunoassay has good reproducibility for PSA detection. The relative standard deviation (RSD%) was found to be 8.52% for 10 independent electrodes and was estimated via the detection of 10 pg mL−1 PSA with identical procedures, indicating good reproducibility.

To evaluate the selectivity of the immunosensor for PSA detection, Fig. 5 compares the ECL responses to some potential interfering proteins, including BSA, CEA, AFP, and mouse IgG solutions (1.0 pg mL−1) under the same experimental conditions. No obvious change in the ECL signals was observed compared with those of 100 fg mL−1 PSA. Similarly, a mixed sample (10 pg mL−1 PSA coexisting with 1.0 pg mL−1 AFP, BSA, CEA and mouse IgG) did not show a remarkable signal change compared with that of PSA alone, demonstrating that the proposed ECL immunoassay has excellent selectivity with regard to PSA detection.

Finally, to evaluate the application and reliability of the proposed ECL immunoassay strategy, the PSA concentration in 6 human serum samples from the Affiliated Hospital of Yantai University was detected. The results obtained using the proposed method displayed a deviation (absolute value) range of 2.40–9.89% from the values provided by the hospital and obtained using the chemiluminescence method. These results illustrated that this ECL immunoassay method was credible to the clinical assay, exhibiting great promise as a reliable technique for the detection of PSA in serum samples.

Highly enhanced ECL intensity from GQD-infilled TiO2 NT electrodes has been reported for the first time. Compared with a pure TiO2 NT electrode, the GQD/TiO2 NT electrode enhanced the ECL intensity by 6-fold and moved the onset of the ECL potential to a more positive position by approximately 300 mV, with an ECL emission that is highly stable and repeatable. Benefiting from these properties, a facile strategy for the fabrication of a sandwich ECL immunoassay was developed using the composite as an efficient ECL emitter. Such an immunosensor demonstrated a high sensitivity and low detection limit for PSA detection and also exhibited good selectivity for PSA detection. Sample assays of PSA in human serum samples confirmed the reliability and practicality of the protocol, demonstrating the potential of this platform for biological sample analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405131, 21778047, 21675138) for support.
